because
your heart is racing, or is your heart racing
because
you feel afraid?

This perspective argues that what we call "emotion" is actually the brain’s attempt to make sense of a sudden shift in our internal "weather"—the raw interoceptive data streaming from our heart, lungs, and gut.
They suggested that emotions are not the
cause
of bodily changes, but the
result
of them.
In this view, your brain does not "detect" emotions in the world or even in your body. Instead, it
constructs
them.
Interoceptive Data (Affect):
The raw, non-specific signals of arousal (high vs. low) and valence (pleasant vs. unpleasant).
Context:
The external environment and the immediate situation you are in.
Past Experience:
Your "database" of previous moments that felt similar.

This area is highly active during almost every emotional experience. Whether you are feeling the "warmth" of compassion or the "chill" of social exclusion, the anterior insula is the place where your physiology is "read" and assigned a meaning.
You begin to realize that the "knot" in your stomach isn't "anxiety"—it’s just a knot in your stomach. The
anxiety
is the story your brain is telling about the knot.

When you are in a state of chronic stress, your brain’s "prediction engine" becomes biased. It starts to see every high-arousal signal as a threat. It constructs "Anxiety" or "Rage" because those are the most "protective" categories it has. Part of your practice is "cleaning the lens" of the anterior insula, so that it can see bodily signals for what they actually are, rather than what it fears they might be.

As you move through your day, Aarish, start to "catch" your brain in the act of construction. When a strong emotion arises, ask: "What is the raw data underneath this label? Is my heart just racing because I had too much coffee, or am I actually 'anxious'?" This simple act of interoceptive inquiry is the first step in reclaiming the "remote control" of your nervous system.

Before the conscious mind registers "I am angry" or "I am overwhelmed," the body has been sending out a series of subtle physiological flickers for minutes, sometimes even hours. The difference between a person who remains regulated and a person who spirals often comes down to one variable: the
lead time
provided by their interoceptive awareness.
Before you reach full sympathetic hyper-arousal (the "activated edge"), there are precursors:
A subtle shift in the pH of the blood as breathing becomes slightly more shallow.
A tiny increase in muscle tone in the small of the back or the jaw.
A microscopic change in the "internal weather"—perhaps a faint buzzing in the hands or a slight cooling of the skin.
The "Interoceptive Widens the Window" effect depends heavily on a concept called
Interoceptive Granularity
Low Granularity:

You feel "bad" or "weird" or "tight." These are global, non-specific labels. Because the data is vague, the brain often defaults to a "high-threat" interpretation, assuming that "vague bad feeling" equals "imminent danger."
High Granularity:
You feel "a localized constriction in the upper left quadrant of the chest, accompanied by a slight upward pressure in the esophagus and a cooling sensation in the temples."
Why does this level of detail matter? Because high-resolution data is
manageable
When the brain receives a vague "global alarm," it triggers a massive autonomic response. But when the Anterior Insula receives highly specific, granular data, it allows for
Inhibitory Control

. The Prefrontal Cortex can look at that specific chest tightening and say, "Ah, that is a familiar sensation associated with social anxiety. It is just a prediction error based on a past experience. I can breathe into that specific spot."
The more "threat" the system perceives, the less "thinking" it allows.
Interoception acts as a buffer by providing the brain with "evidence of safety" even in the midst of activation.
This second stream allows the Prefrontal Cortex to stay "online" much longer than it otherwise would. This is the
Ventral-Sympathetic Blend
we touched on in Topic 3.
A feeling of "forward leaning" in your consciousness, as if you are mentally reaching for the next moment rather than being in this one.
A subtle blurring of the visual field or a loss of peripheral awareness.

By sharpening the resolution of your "internal map" (the Insula), you are literally building the structural capacity to handle more of life. You aren't just learning to "relax"; you are upgrading the hardware that determines how much intensity you can hold before you break.
We must understand that
regulation follows perception
. You cannot regulate what you do not notice.
This shift from "reaction" to "investigation" is the hallmark of a wide Window of Tolerance. It turns the nervous system from a rigid, brittle structure into a flexible, resilient landscape.
This is the essence of nervous system mastery: the ability to feel everything, so you can choose how to respond to anything.
But the goal of these practices isn’t just to "calm down"—it is to recalibrate the hardware.
reframing these practices as
neurological sensor training
changes everything.
Hebb’s Rule:
neurons that fire together, wire together.
Over time, the "white matter" (the cabling) connecting your body’s sensors to the insula becomes thicker and more insulated, allowing data to travel faster and with less "noise."

You might feel a global sense of "dread" (High Anterior activity) without actually being able to pinpoint the physical sensations in the body that are fueling it (Low Posterior resolution). This is how the "anxiety gap" forms: the brain makes up a scary story because it can't read the actual data.
In yoga, you aren't just sensing the body at rest; you are sensing the body under
controlled stress

You are intentionally keeping the Ventral Vagal system online while the Sympathetic system is firing. To do this, the insula must work overtime. It has to process the "noise" of the muscle burn while simultaneously monitoring the "signal" of the slow, rhythmic breath.
In physical training, the growth happens at the "failure point" of the set. In interoceptive training, the growth happens at the
edges of sensation

When your interoceptive sensor is "blurry," you are more likely to fall into "groupthink" or reactionary behavior because you cannot distinguish your own authentic somatic markers from the "social noise" around you.

By training your insula to be high-resolution, you are essentially strengthening your "moral compass." You become better at sensing when an action or a group consensus "doesn't sit right" in your body—long before you can explain it logically. This is the biological basis for integrity.
Gray matter is where the processing happens—it is the "CPU power" of the brain. By doing the work, you are physically expanding the processor that manages your Window of Tolerance.

It allows the "thinking brain" to receive the body's data faster and more clearly, which enables you to use "Top-Down" strategies (like cognitive reframing) much more effectively. You don't just "feel" the stress; you have the "bandwidth" to decide what to do about it.
your heart is a high-performance engine that naturally wants to "idle" at around 100 BPM. This is known as the
intrinsic heart rate
, governed by the heart's internal pacemaker, the sinoatrial (SA) node.
the Nucleus Ambiguus is never silent. It is constantly firing inhibitory signals—essentially saying "slow down, slow down, slow down"—to the SA node.
How does a nerve signal actually "brake" a physical muscle like the heart? It uses a neurotransmitter called
acetylcholine

. When the vagal signal reaches the SA node, it releases acetylcholine, which acts like a cooling mist on the heart's electrical system. It increases the time between each "spark," thereby lowering the heart rate.
The goal of regulation is often to keep the brake partially engaged, even when the heart rate is high—a state we call a
Ventral-Sympathetic blend
When you inhale, the chest cavity expands, and a complex feedback loop tells the brainstem to temporarily inhibit the vagus nerve. For those few seconds, the Nucleus Ambiguus "lets off the brake."
A healthy heart is a "jittery" heart. It is a heart that is constantly listening to the lungs, speeding up slightly on the inhale and slowing down significantly on the exhale. This
Heart Rate Variability (HRV)
—which we will dive into in the next part—is the external measurement of how well your vagal brake is working.
By measuring the tiny differences in time between each heartbeat, we can calculate
Heart Rate Variability (HRV)
Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia (RSA)
is the natural oscillation of the heart rate with the breath—inhale speeds up, exhale slows down.

In a healthy, resilient body, the heart is not a metronome; it is more like a jazz drummer. It is constantly adjusting, speeding up and slowing down by tiny fractions of a second with every single breath and every shift in thought. This variation is known as
Heart Rate Variability (HRV)
The
RMSSD
(Root Mean Square of Successive Differences) is the most important metric for our purposes. It looks at the "successive" differences—the change from one beat to the the very next beat.
SDNN
(Standard Deviation of NN intervals) measures the variability over a longer period—often a full 24 hours or during a 5-minute recording.
While a high ratio (more LF than HF) can suggest sympathetic dominance, it's better to think of it as a measure of
complexity

. A healthy system should have energy in both frequencies. If the ratio becomes extremely skewed, it signals that the nervous system is no longer "playing the full keyboard" of its regulatory capacity.
If your HRV is trending downward over a week, it is a signal from your body that your Window of Tolerance is shrinking. It’s an early warning that you are using up your "regulatory reserves."
For the nervous system, sleep is the primary period for
autonomic recalibration
This happens because, during deep sleep, the SNS effectively goes offline, and the
myelinated vagus
(the "modern" vagus we discussed in Polyvagal Theory) takes full control of the heart.
Poor sleep is the fastest, most reliable way to suppress the vagal brake and shrink your Window of Tolerance the following day.
For most people, alcohol suppresses the vagal brake for 12 to 24 hours, meaning your "regulatory reserve" is significantly lower the entire next day.

However, it does not "repair" the brake. If your HRV is low because of poor sleep, using caffeine to "power through" simply masks the fatigue while keeping your SNS in a state of high activation. This increases your
allostatic load
(the wear-and-tear of stress), making it more likely that you will experience a "crash" or an emotional "snap" once the caffeine wears off and the underlying lack of vagal tone is revealed.
Exercise is, by definition, a
sympathetic stressor
Chronic high-load training without sufficient recovery leads to a
suppressed HRV baseline
Each of these prevents the vagal brake from ever fully engaging. Over time, this leads to
Baseline Drift
. Your nervous system "forgets" how to reach its highest HRV potential. You might feel "fine," but your hardware is stuck in a high-RPM resting state.
From a neurological perspective, being in "sync" with a safe social group is incredibly efficient. When you feel a sense of belonging and safety with others, your
Social Engagement System
(the Ventral Vagus) is active.
Conversely, social isolation or "masking" (pretending to be someone you aren't to fit in) is a significant "leak" in your HRV bank account.
By adjusting your "load" based on your "reserve," you avoid the trap of allostatic overload and begin the process of structural expansion—slowly but surely raising your baseline HRV over time.

When these three systems synchronize, they create a "constructive interference" pattern. The peaks and valleys of your heart rate don’t just happen; they grow larger. This state is what researchers call
Resonance Frequency Breathing (RFB)

In a stressed or "incoherent" state, your breath and your baroreflex are often at odds. You might be inhaling (which tells the heart to speed up) just as your baroreflex is trying to slow the heart down to manage a pressure spike. This creates "physiological noise"—it’s like trying to drive a car with one foot on the gas and the other on the brake, alternating rapidly and chaotically.

When you achieve this resonance, your HRV waveform transforms. If you were looking at a real-time graph of your heart rate (the "tachogram"), you would see a shift from a jagged, chaotic line to a smooth, elegant
sine wave
. This state is known as
Cardiac Coherence
Coherence
is a state of
high-order synchronization
. It is a Ventral Vagal state (Topic 3.3) where you are alert, present, and physiologically efficient.
When your heart rhythm becomes coherent, your nervous system becomes a "biological laser." You aren't necessarily "more" relaxed; you are more
integrated
Resonance frequency breathing isn't just a "break" from your day; it is a
deliberate training session for the myelinated vagus.

Think of your Window of Tolerance as a physical structure. Chronic stress and "incoherent" living make the walls of that window brittle and narrow. Vagal power-lifting—deliberately driving the system into high-amplitude resonance—increases the "flexibility" of those walls.
You aren't just practicing being "calm" in a dark room; you are training the hardware of your nervous system to be able to
return
to calm from anywhere on the Polyvagal ladder.
The goal is to move from
functional regulation
(doing it when you have to) to
structural resilience
(changing the baseline of your nervous system).

The same logic applies to your nervous system. When you finish a twenty-minute session of resonance frequency breathing or a deep body scan, you feel a shift. Your heart rate slows, your shoulders drop, and you feel more "in" your window. This is a
functional shift
—a temporary change in state.
Structural Vagal Tone

is like upgrading the insulation in the walls and installing a more powerful, responsive heating and cooling system. When we talk about building structural tone, we are talking about long-term, durable changes in the "vagal hardware."
If functional tone is a "reset," structural tone is an "upgrade." You aren't just coming back to the window; you are widening the window itself.
While your heart rate changes in seconds, the structural remodeling of the brain and autonomic pathways takes time.
8-to-12 week window as the "tipping point" for structural change.

Think of the Nucleus Ambiguus (NA) as the "muscle" that holds the vagal brake. In the beginning, holding that brake during a stressful shadow work session or a difficult yoga pose feels like a massive effort of will. But as structural tone builds, the NA becomes "stronger."
Aerobic fitness and cardiovascular health provide the
physical infrastructure
for high HRV. This creates a powerful synergy with your contemplative practices.
This matters for the vagus because of
arterial compliance

. If your arteries are supple and your heart is efficient, the baroreceptors (pressure sensors) in your neck and chest send "cleaner" signals to the brain. When the vagus nerve wants to slow the heart down, an elastic, healthy heart responds to that "instruction" more fluidly.
the Autonomic Nervous System follows the law of
Supercompensation

. When you stress the system (through a hard workout or a cold plunge) and then follow it with a period of deep, intentional recovery, the system doesn't just return to baseline—it overshoots it. It builds back slightly stronger.
This domain includes the "classic" five senses: sight, hearing, touch, taste, and smell. It is your nervous system’s way of scanning the environment.
Interoceptive Accuracy refers to your objective ability to accurately detect internal bodily signals. This is the "hardware" performance of your nervous system. It isn't about how you
feel
about your body; it’s about whether you can correctly identify the data being sent from your organs to your brain.
Interoceptive Sensibility is your self-reported tendency to focus on internal sensations and your
belief
in your own sensitivity. This is usually measured via questionnaires like the
MAIA
(Multidimensional Assessment of Interoceptive Awareness).
The third layer is the most sophisticated: Interoceptive Awareness (sometimes called Interoceptive Metacognition). This is the degree to which your confidence matches your actual performance.
Predictive Processing
suggests that your "felt sense" of being alive is actually the brain’s best guess about the state of your body, updated by sensory data.
This is the raw data traveling up the Vagus nerve and spinal cord that we mapped in Part 4.2. It says:
"Actually, the heart rate is steady and the breath is deep."
The system undergoes temporary dissonance.
Perceptual Inference:
The brain changes its model. (e.g., "Oh, I guess I'm safer than I thought. Let's slow the heart rate down.")
Active Inference:
The brain changes the body to match the model. (e.g., "My model says I'm in danger, but my heart is slow. I will
force
the heart to beat faster to match my fear.")
Your lungs are not just two big balloons; they are made up of approximately 500 million tiny, grape-like air sacs called
alveoli

This creates a feedback loop: stress causes shallow breathing, which causes alveoli to collapse, which causes CO2 to rise, which tells the brain you are in danger, which increases your stress response.
The second half of the physiological sigh is the
extended exhale
. As we explored in Topic 5, your heart and your lungs are inextricably linked through a process called
Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia (RSA)
The result? Your heart rate drops almost immediately. The brain receives a "bottom-up" report:
The alveoli are open, the CO2 is gone, and the heart rate is slowing down. We must be safe.

The physiological sigh, however, doesn't care if you are "good" at meditating. It is a biological lever. By mechanically popping open the alveoli and forcing a vagal brake engagement, it bypasses the "thinking brain" (PFC) and talks directly to the "survival brain" (brainstem).
Atelectasis (Alveoli Collapse):
Stress causes shallow breathing, leading to collapsed air sacs and trapped CO2, which signals "danger" to the brain.
The Double-Inhale:
The second "sniff" provides the pressure needed to re-inflate these sacs, maximizing surface area for CO2 offloading.
Vagal Braking:
The long exhale following the double-inhale is the primary driver of the heart rate drop via the vagus nerve.
By changing the ratio of your breath, you are not just relaxing; you are manually overriding the default settings of your autonomic hardware.
the heart is also responding to a series of pressure changes dictated by the
diaphragm
Inside the arch of your aorta and the carotid sinus are
baroreceptors
—specialized pressure-sensing neurons.
Result:
Inhale → Lower Pressure → Vagal Withdrawal → Heart Rate Increases.
Result:
Exhale → Higher Pressure → Vagal Engagement → Heart Rate Decreases.

once you understand the mechanics of RSA, you realize that you can shift the "net tone" of your nervous system simply by changing the math of the breath cycle. If you spend more time in the "braking" phase (exhale) than the "acceleration" phase (inhale), you are sending a sustained, repetitive signal of safety and deceleration to the brain.
By extending the exhale, you are effectively "flooding" the heart with acetylcholine for a larger percentage of every minute. This forces the
Allostatic baseline
to shift downward, widening your Window of Tolerance in the moment by reducing the background "noise" of sympathetic activation.
The Physiological Sigh is a
Structural Reset

. It is designed to pop open collapsed alveoli and offload a massive "dump" of carbon dioxide. It is an emergency tool. If you are in a state of high hyper-arousal—say, mid-panic attack or in a moment of intense rage—the Physiological Sigh is your fastest way to break the circuit. It is a "reset" for the hardware.
The
Extended Exhale
, by contrast, is a
Tuning Tool
. It is less about a sudden "pop" and more about the gradual "steering" of your state over several minutes.
By maintaining a 1:2 ratio
while
you engage with the difficult material, you are creating a
Ventral-Sympathetic Blend
You are no longer just "trying to relax"; you are actively watching your brainstem manipulate your heart's pacemaker in response to your breath.
Increase the "gain" of the baroreflex:
Your body becomes more sensitive and efficient at regulating blood pressure and heart rate.
Increase myelination:
The "insulation" on your vagal fibers may strengthen, allowing for faster and more precise "braking."
It often feels like you are being "breathed" by the rhythm rather than doing the work yourself. This is a sign that the mechanical and biological rhythms have synchronized.
When your body is in a state of coherence, the "bottom-up" data being sent to your brain is incredibly clear and rhythmic. This makes it much easier for your brain to update its internal models.
Resonance Frequency Breathing represents a shift in how we think about regulation. It’s not just about "less" (less stress, less heart rate). It’s about
harmony
You may have heard of this technique in the context of high-stakes performance—it is famously used by Navy SEALs, first responders, and elite athletes.
Box Breathing is defined by its 1:1:1:1 ratio. Typically, this is practiced as a 4-second inhale, a 4-second hold, a 4-second exhale, and a 4-second hold.
This phase trains the system to remain calm while "loaded" with potential energy.
It prevents the system from "crashing" into a state of collapse, maintaining a thread of attention throughout the transition.

This phase often triggers a "neuroceptive" threat signal—the brain wonders if you’ve forgotten to breathe. By staying still in this "void," you are teaching your nervous system that "emptiness" or "lack of input" is not an emergency.
The most unique physiological feature of Box Breathing is not the breathing itself, but the
breath retention.
Your brain is actually quite bad at detecting low oxygen (hypoxia). Instead, it is hyper-sensitive to the buildup of carbon dioxide (hypercapnia).

When you perform the "holds" in the box, you are deliberately allowing CO2 to rise. By doing this repeatedly in a controlled, safe environment (the "Ventral Vagal anchor"), you are performing a form of
desensitization.
You are showing the brainstem that a rise in CO2 does not mean death is imminent.

Because the ratio is 1:1, it doesn't push you all the way into "sleepy/relaxed" mode. Instead, it "brackets" your arousal. It pulls you from the edges of the Window of Tolerance and moves you toward the exact center.
This state is often called
"Calm Readiness."
You are "Ventral" (safe and connected), but you have enough "Sympathetic" tone to remain alert and responsive.
in the
Kumbhaka
—the moments of absolute breathlessness—the "chatter" of the mind (the
Vritti
) naturally tends to go quiet.
Amygdala / Hypothalamus Triggered
Top Down Signal Dispatched
Vagal Nerve Streams (afferent) Data to NTS
NTS Confirms Threat
Send Data to Thalamus and Hypothalamus
Triggers Sympathetic Dominanace
Dosrsal Downshift
Active Regulation (Triggering Ventral Brakes)
The Relational Domain—Social Engagement System
Internal Domain—Interoception
Exteroception—Outside World
Top-Down Prediction (Mental Model)
Bottom-Up Signals (The Raw Data)
Resolution Strategy

projects"}]}]},{"type":"toggle_list_item","attrs":{"id":"ce3f9e16-fc4e-43ec-8587-d978bac956df","folded":true},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"aed231ff-0fb9-488d-82cc-9aa680c36f16"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Are there more real-world use cases of how others use Heptabase? "}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"206f3bc3-5fc6-4d5c-8eac-2e2b08dce47e"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"In the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"link","attrs":{"href":"https://heptabase.com/gallery","title":null,"data-internal-href":null,"edited":false}}],"text":"Heptabase Gallery"},{"type":"text","text":", We curate and showcase many whiteboards from our users, where you can see how people use Heptabase for learning, research, and planning."}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"9ce70df1-dab0-4843-a2ad-9e0c0ec472c9"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"When you’ve created a whiteboard you like, you’re welcome to share it through "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"link","attrs":{"href":"https://form.typeform.com/to/uNKKn2Gq","title":null,"data-internal-href":null,"edited":false}}],"text":"this link"},{"type":"text","text":" at any time. After it passes review, your whiteboard will be published so people can discover and learn from it!"}]}]},{"type":"toggle_list_item","attrs":{"id":"7a3c9956-780e-42ed-9f6a-2abb40884aad","folded":true},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"81ba613f-e371-474f-802f-fbdeb62f1c26"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"When to use tags vs. whiteboards?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"6eb6335e-42fb-41cc-a7c3-3c3dfa46b911"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Our official suggestion: Use whiteboards for "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"knowledge and idea"},{"type":"text","text":" cards (e.g., book notes, research notes). Only consider using tags when you have a large number of "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"pure record/archive"},{"type":"text","text":" cards (e.g., meeting notes, literature). Since Heptabase is designed for learning and researching complex topics, in 95% of cases we recommend using whiteboards."}]}]}]}

cardList.title: Course Roadmap
cardList.content: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"heading","attrs":{"id":"cb78609f-b7d1-40ff-a7bd-db31d3d0f349","level":1},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Course Roadmap"}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"5cfe32a7-b30a-46b3-b424-ac7f0e3472ec"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"You likely know the feeling of being \"on your game.\" Perhaps it’s during a meditation session where the mind is quiet, yet alert; or during a heavy lifting set where your focus is laser-sharp and your body feels like a perfectly tuned machine. "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"highlight","attrs":{"ids":["b2112df5-e12e-40c6-af64-4496583c52fc"]}}],"text":"In these moments, you are residing in a state of physiological and psychological flow."},{"type":"text","text":" But you also know the opposite: the sudden spike of irritation during a stressful meeting, the brain fog that sets in after a week of overwork, or the \"shut down\" feeling where motivation evaporates and you feel disconnected from your goals."}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"0c3a3d9b-0a5c-464c-8a64-54be8f937e80"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"The central question of this course is simple but profound: "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"Why does your internal state shift so drastically, and how can you move the needle back toward balance with precision?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"b6c5f82a-095f-4190-be03-7a0e6cb5b9a9"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"You already have a rich toolkit—breathwork, yoga, meditation, and physical training. You’ve felt these practices work. This course is designed to show you "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"how"},{"type":"text","text":" they work by pulling back the curtain on the neuroscience and physiology of your nervous system. We aren’t just looking for \"relaxation.\" "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"highlight","attrs":{"ids":["c4401906-0708-4a10-aa11-bfb0db15642d"]}}],"text":"We are looking for "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"highlight","attrs":{"ids":["c4401906-0708-4a10-aa11-bfb0db15642d"]}},{"type":"strong"}],"text":"regulation"},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"highlight","attrs":{"ids":["c4401906-0708-4a10-aa11-bfb0db15642d"]}}],"text":"—the ability to stay flexible, resilient, and present, even when the world gets loud."}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"ebdcd70f-70e1-4c5a-984f-c2b6e74b1ee4"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"To do this, we will move through four foundational pillars. Each pillar builds on the last, taking you from a high-level map of your experience down into the cellular machinery of your body, and finally back out to the specific techniques you can use every day."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"id":"246e90e0-612b-4fa0-9e66-b220745f862f","level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Pillar 1: The Window of Tolerance (The Conceptual Map)"}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"b1f6dfee-34d4-490f-9770-3d06646c0e73"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Before we can fix a system, we need a map of its territory. The first pillar of this course is the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"Window of Tolerance"},{"type":"text","text":". This concept, originally developed by Dr. Dan Siegel, serves as our North Star. "}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"c7b2d062-429f-4fd5-a398-d6b72c873b7d"},"content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"highlight","attrs":{"ids":["39c5f0ed-459b-4205-9611-4c77cb405b52"]}}],"text":"Think of your nervous system’s arousal level as a fluctuating wave. When you are inside your \"Window,\" you are in a state of optimal arousal."},{"type":"text","text":" "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"highlight","attrs":{"ids":["fbce9df1-7be8-4476-a52e-a6cda5e50233"]}}],"text":"You can process information effectively, you feel emotionally balanced, and you have access to your higher-level thinking."},{"type":"text","text":" You aren’t necessarily \"calm\"—you could be sprinting a 5k or engaged in a heated intellectual debate—but you are "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"regulated"},{"type":"text","text":". You are in the driver's seat."}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"87de5c55-3b34-41f0-b32a-320f7a7a959a"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"However, when life pushes us too hard, we hit the edges of this window. "}]},{"type":"bullet_list_item","attrs":{"id":"e0152f57-0007-4f79-b362-f692174669c6","folded":false,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"44c0794a-3050-4273-9f41-b369ed848b84"},"content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"The Upper Edge (Hyper-arousal):"},{"type":"text","text":" This is the \"fight or flight\" zone. It’s characterized by anxiety, racing thoughts, and physical tension. "}]}]},{"type":"bullet_list_item","attrs":{"id":"584d4f1d-dd0f-48ee-b512-2b5e1e0223fa","folded":false,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"8da0f55f-c8d9-4ba8-9c4f-70875611948e"},"content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"The Lower Edge (Hypo-arousal):"},{"type":"text","text":" This is the \"shutdown\" zone. It feels like numbness, exhaustion, or a lack of \"will.\""}]}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"f294b180-9646-412e-8fab-cd5c14e67f3a"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"In this first section of the course, we will explore why your window is the size it is. You might find that your shadow work or meditation practice has already started to widen this window, allowing you to stay present with difficult emotions that would have previously sent you into a tailspin. We will define the \"edges\" of your personal landscape so that you can recognize them the moment you begin to drift toward them."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"id":"8205b9c1-57de-42f7-87f6-c41bf6ff93ee","level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Pillar 2: The Autonomic Nervous System (The Hardware)"}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"8c5fb8f0-59d4-4031-95e9-2ded8af9933b"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Once we have the map, we need to look at the hardware that generates the terrain: the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"Autonomic Nervous System (ANS)"},{"type":"text","text":". If the Window of Tolerance is the \"what,\" the ANS is the \"how.\""}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"b6b3042d-4a16-434b-9d19-572f98a7b13e"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Most people have heard of the Sympathetic (fight/flight) and Parasympathetic (rest/digest) branches. But in this pillar, we go deeper. "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"highlight","attrs":{"ids":["ab70b451-a741-4eaf-823d-adec2870af8f"]}}],"text":"We will look at the ANS not as a simple on/off switch, but as a complex biological gearbox. Every breath you take and every movement you make is a signal to this system."}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"d53f2107-59d4-43a9-91e0-b6ac91e91162"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"This is where your "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"breathwork and yoga"},{"type":"text","text":" practices begin to make scientific sense. When you perform a specific pranayama (breathing technique), you aren't just \"calming your mind\"; you are literally hacking the electrical signals traveling from your lungs to your brain. You are shifting gears in your biological hardware. We will explore the anatomy of the Vagus nerve—the \"master cable\" of the parasympathetic system—and understand how it connects your brain to your heart, gut, and lungs. By the end of this pillar, you’ll see your physical training not just as muscular work, but as a high-intensity stress test for your nervous system's ability to recover."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"id":"98395902-0af6-49af-840a-f93a9da1ff07","level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Pillar 3: Interoception (The Sensory Feed)"}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"cd9db592-6571-42f5-ae8c-e88b1b246487"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"The third pillar is perhaps the most overlooked skill in performance and well-being: "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"Interoception"},{"type":"text","text":". While exteroception is your sense of the outside world (sight, sound, touch), "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"highlight","attrs":{"ids":["a63970a4-cfea-4ec9-bed1-86f7e1445d6f"]}}],"text":"interoception is your \"eighth sense\"—it is your brain’s perception of the internal state of your body."}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"dba90467-0403-4693-a7c6-95271c27a4e1"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"How do you "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"know"},{"type":"text","text":" you are getting angry before the first word leaves your mouth? How do you "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"know"},{"type":"text","text":" you are reaching your physical limit during a workout? The answer is interoception. Your brain is constantly receiving a stream of data from your organs: the rate of your heartbeat, the acidity of your blood, the tension in your fascia."}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"73de8127-f45d-4a17-a9f1-4b547fe05a75"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"For someone with a dedicated "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"body scan or yoga practice"},{"type":"text","text":", this pillar will feel like coming home. We will explore the \"Insular Cortex,\" the part of the brain that serves as the internal dashboard for these signals. "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"highlight","attrs":{"ids":["7d12337a-6deb-4c54-9962-bd22b41e9b7e"]}}],"text":"We’ll discuss why some people are \"body-blind\" (low interoceptive accuracy) and how this leads to sudden, unexpected emotional outbursts or \"crashing.\""},{"type":"text","text":" Conversely, we will see how building interoceptive awareness—learning to \"read the gauges\"—is the secret to widening your Window of Tolerance. "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"highlight","attrs":{"ids":["6892f267-2cc3-457c-ba79-c7f84da6d434"]}}],"text":"You can't regulate what you don't notice."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"id":"e516caf6-3696-4157-b874-e8a2e2f41eb9","level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Pillar 4: Vagal Tone and Down-Regulation (The Toolkit)"}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"3f8adc8f-945a-432f-bfb3-e8450750039b"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"The final pillar is where theory becomes practice. We take everything we’ve learned about the map, the hardware, and the sensors, and we turn them into a "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"Down-Regulation Toolkit"},{"type":"text","text":". "}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"8a8d75c0-5ab4-4992-b594-df493f1ad1ed"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"In this section, we focus on "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"Vagal Tone"},{"type":"text","text":". Just as you can train your biceps or your cardiovascular endurance, you can train the \"strength\" and \"flexibility\" of your vagus nerve. "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"highlight","attrs":{"ids":["33ee9efc-7211-4bca-a85a-19cab12d4a69"]}}],"text":"A high vagal tone means your body can pivot from a state of high stress back to a state of calm almost instantly. "}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"5806a07e-420a-4651-a742-643c5593843e"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"This is where we integrate your "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"cold exposure and high-intensity training"},{"type":"text","text":". "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"highlight","attrs":{"ids":["a8328502-e3bf-419a-b0d1-a730eed00d60"]}}],"text":"We will look at why jumping into a cold plunge isn't just about \"mental toughness\"—it’s a deliberate provocation of the \"mammalian diving reflex,\" a hard-wired biological brake that forces your nervous system into a parasympathetic state."},{"type":"text","text":" We will explore:"}]},{"type":"bullet_list_item","attrs":{"id":"54833065-590b-4082-aa5b-b696803f284a","folded":false,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"61a35282-805e-4e36-9021-3e4f299549fb"},"content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"The Physiological Sigh:"},{"type":"text","text":" A specific breathing pattern that is the fastest known way to offload CO2 and lower heart rate."}]}]},{"type":"bullet_list_item","attrs":{"id":"b9f23294-ad2d-450d-a824-f5a1b65f8bca","folded":false,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"6b1e28bb-64c5-4f8a-a245-1d9aef0f8bf7"},"content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"Resonance Frequency Breathing:"},{"type":"text","text":" Finding the exact \"rhythm\" where your heart and lungs sync up to maximize regulation."}]}]},{"type":"bullet_list_item","attrs":{"id":"b606142e-5a68-4a49-81a0-49411337badd","folded":false,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"42afe2aa-f7fa-434c-869c-93b7b0b56a89"},"content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"Somatic Movement:"},{"type":"text","text":" Using the body to \"complete\" stress cycles that the mind hasn't let go of yet."}]}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"id":"f85784eb-5347-4ef6-901a-5c741d956b24","level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"The Logic of the Build: Why This Sequence?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"9cfaa4c9-d966-4e86-9ac3-45a7c5c91f20"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"You might wonder why we don’t start with the tools. Why not jump straight to the \"three breaths to stop a panic attack\"? "}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"1d53a00f-888c-49e5-8031-390c3bfd6afb"},"content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"highlight","attrs":{"ids":["43e8830b-88f3-4188-a536-bdb406074d93"]}}],"text":"The reason is that "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"highlight","attrs":{"ids":["43e8830b-88f3-4188-a536-bdb406074d93"]}},{"type":"strong"}],"text":"tools without context are fragile."},{"type":"text","text":" If you try a breathing exercise but don’t understand the \"gearbox\" (Pillar 2), you might get frustrated if it doesn’t work instantly. If you don’t have a \"map\" (Pillar 1), you won't even know "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"which"},{"type":"text","text":" tool to reach for. Is this a moment that requires a \"brake\" (down-regulation), or is it a moment where you are in hypo-arousal and actually need a \"gas pedal\" (up-regulation)?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"1543d7f7-5b3f-424e-b719-0dafbcfd73c2"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"By following this sequence, we move from the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"conceptual"},{"type":"text","text":" to the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"physiological"},{"type":"text","text":", then to the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"perceptual"},{"type":"text","text":", and finally to the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"practical"},{"type":"text","text":". "}]},{"type":"numbered_list_item","attrs":{"id":"1a79577c-1b5c-4ee8-8f41-3daf3a20f081","order":null,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"5f962444-0703-4697-9ebb-2797cba2d27f"},"content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"Framework (Window of Tolerance):"},{"type":"text","text":" We establish the goal. What does success look like? (Staying in the window)."}]}]},{"type":"numbered_list_item","attrs":{"id":"e2cce099-0d3a-4d7f-ad17-7e921d4ac72e","order":null,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"90a7fa0f-7f2d-4bc6-9b17-de168681e830"},"content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"Hardware (ANS):"},{"type":"text","text":" We understand the machine we are working with. Why does the body react the way it does?"}]}]},{"type":"numbered_list_item","attrs":{"id":"7de4a1e0-9e26-4b93-bf16-9f810cb317f5","order":null,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"057137c2-29f2-4198-8d78-9d539ac36a14"},"content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"Perception (Interoception):"},{"type":"text","text":" We install the software. How do I detect my state in real-time?"}]}]},{"type":"numbered_list_item","attrs":{"id":"1089538f-de01-4216-9463-1cf9ea7c7eb1","order":null,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"fca7baed-15d6-4662-8b4c-2d69aa9e3858"},"content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"Tools (Down-Regulation):"},{"type":"text","text":" We master the controls. How do I move my state back to where I want it?"}]}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"id":"7730058c-a7eb-41dc-af60-4b62a978d1d3","level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Connecting to Your Existing Practice"}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"93634edb-cca3-4194-bb5a-5a5b54bab828"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Aarish, you aren't starting from scratch. You are already an \"intuitive engineer\" of your own nervous system. "}]},{"type":"bullet_list_item","attrs":{"id":"b93465c0-d3a2-44f9-ad08-4fef063fd8bf","folded":false,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"d2b6dfd3-f6e8-4b72-bab6-6dc5c5d9f7c7"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"When you sit for "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"meditation"},{"type":"text","text":", you are practicing Pillar 1 (witnessing the edges of your window) and Pillar 3 (noticing internal sensations). "}]}]},{"type":"bullet_list_item","attrs":{"id":"a00447dc-7dd2-4dd1-acf1-7d7a0644cd28","folded":false,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"1cfdef3c-562c-4d5d-8de2-646c5d4a17f8"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"When you do "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"shadow work"},{"type":"text","text":", you are testing the boundaries of your Window of Tolerance, learning to hold space for \"high-charge\" material without hitting the hyper-arousal ceiling. "}]}]},{"type":"bullet_list_item","attrs":{"id":"e9d57dce-a4d1-4901-859d-b83e4c9445ba","folded":false,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"bea9e99b-e611-44cb-9759-eb4d2c1472fb"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"When you engage in "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"physical training"},{"type":"text","text":", you are exercising the hardware of Pillar 2, pushing the sympathetic system so that the parasympathetic \"rebound\" becomes stronger."}]}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"8bd0064f-ba8c-4c47-ac46-f02ee63f85fe"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"This course is the bridge. It provides the technical vocabulary and the \"why\" behind the \"what\" you’ve been doing for years. Instead of just feeling that yoga makes you feel \"better,\" you will understand that the slow, mindful transitions are increasing your interoceptive accuracy and \"toning\" the ventral vagal circuit of your brain. "}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"id":"98ff2167-4962-4e9d-ab09-9e34400e9004","level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"The Central Goal: Deliberate Capacity"}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"7085b72e-f3e3-46da-a2dc-105b08d880c0"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Ultimately, this course is about moving from "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"accidental regulation"},{"type":"text","text":" (feeling good because you had a \"good day\") to "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"deliberate regulation"},{"type":"text","text":" (being able to maintain your center because you understand your biology). "}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"fae9e28c-2e41-4d42-a4fe-4f9e039cfdca"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"We are not aiming for a life without stress. That is impossible and, frankly, boring. We are aiming for a life where your Window of Tolerance is so wide and your regulatory tools are so sharp that you can step into high-pressure situations, deep emotional work, or intense physical challenges with the absolute confidence that you can find your way back to center. "}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"44f43a35-b360-4af7-986a-d7fcd0ee98f3"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"We are building a nervous system that is not just \"calm,\" but "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"resilient"},{"type":"text","text":". "}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"id":"ebd05bd2-77e9-4306-9ae0-1da2e00ee269","level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Navigating the Arousal Landscape"}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"21656004-0bc6-4e7b-a6c4-491c3524fdd4"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"As we move forward, keep in mind that the nervous system is not a static thing—it is a living, breathing process. It is constantly \"predicting\" what you need next. If your brain predicts danger, it revs the engine. If it predicts safety, it engages the brake. "}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"6e6d381e-cf54-49c8-a89e-04eb7be35a62"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Our journey begins with the map. In the next section, we will look at how the Window of Tolerance came to be. We will see how a framework originally meant to help people recover from profound trauma became the gold standard for understanding human performance and emotional health. Understanding the \"edges\" of your world is the first step in mastering it."}]},{"type":"horizontal_rule","attrs":{"id":"f88dc05d-e907-4b29-82ce-f941de67268a"}},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"id":"19c63960-8bcb-4351-847b-ef58a2f1bf11","level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Mapping the Journey Ahead"}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"cfae47ca-b595-4b87-aa1f-5f5ab8a6c238"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"In this opening part, we have laid out the four pillars that will define your study of nervous system regulation: the Window of Tolerance as our framework, the Autonomic Nervous System as our biological hardware, Interoception as our internal sensing mechanism, and Vagal Toning as our practical toolkit. We’ve established that your existing practices in meditation, breathwork, and physical training are not separate from this science, but are the very methods through which these physiological systems are trained. By moving from high-level frameworks to specific biological mechanisms, you will build a sophisticated understanding of why your state shifts and how to reclaim it. The next step in this journey is to explore the origin of our primary map—the Window of Tolerance—"},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"highlight","attrs":{"ids":["6a892eeb-be21-4c3f-94fe-6de5f7a48527"]}}],"text":"and understand how it defines the boundary between optimal performance and survival-based reactivity."}]}]}

cardList.title: Get started with Heptabase
cardList.content: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"heading","attrs":{"id":"db80f33f-3939-4437-987d-fb799024526b","level":1},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Get started with Heptabase"}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"e85ed16b-4cb8-42bb-a0bc-b8680d1dfe2f"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Heptabase is designed specifically for "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"students,"},{"type":"text","text":" "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"researchers, and lifelong learners"},{"type":"text","text":". Once it's rich enough, break it into smaller concept cards and arrange them on your whiteboard to visualize their relationships."}]}]},{"type":"toggle_list_item","attrs":{"id":"5438e6a5-e771-4c8d-991d-d793ac27f905","folded":true},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"ad29af60-e563-462e-9e62-9b7ab5c5194b"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Watch how it works"}]},{"type":"video","attrs":{"id":"52d01385-06be-4a03-a8cd-8284b284eb6f","fileId":null,"url":"https://www.youtube.com/embed/HgvR2QkfwG0","source":"video","width":"100%","alignment":"center","originalWidth":null,"originalHeight":null,"reference":null}}]}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"87f6da81-90a3-4182-85b7-e5b91dd6b497"}},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"113674c3-70e5-414a-aa91-67335fc740eb"},"content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"Heptabase currently offers two subscription plans—Pro and Premium:"}]},{"type":"bullet_list_item","attrs":{"id":"4803791d-cb36-437d-bfbe-17f3e30f51f1","folded":false,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"10b3bdc9-7daf-4d32-a579-d0e5f5639e47"},"content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"Premium plan"},{"type":"text","text":": Get 10x monthly AI usage quota of the Pro plan and unlimited PDF uploads. Ideal for users who heavily rely on AI for learning and research and want extensive use of the most advanced AI reasoning models."}]}]},{"type":"bullet_list_item","attrs":{"id":"ca6013fc-84eb-41dd-bd50-a4211c8a498d","folded":false,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"6a72dd93-c0a6-47be-ac9e-b969fc701a57"},"content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"Pro plan"},{"type":"text","text":": Suitable for users who use AI features less frequently and want to use Heptabase as their primary note-taking and knowledge base software."}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"b2ea219b-25f3-421b-afb1-d3f51e663915"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"In fact, even before launching any AI features, Heptabase won the Product Hunt 2023 Personal Productivity Tool of the Year award, making it a visual note-taking software particularly well-suited for students and researchers."}]}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"f1d60be1-8f82-43f4-b207-9b1baaaa7ac9"}},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"fb149a0a-1650-41f3-a501-29de62d705be"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"If you want to learn more about Heptabase, we recommend checking out our "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"link","attrs":{"href":"https://wiki.heptabase.com/","title":null,"data-internal-href":null,"edited":false}}],"text":"official wiki"},{"type":"text","text":". It not only includes tutorials for Heptabase but also "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"link","attrs":{"href":"https://wiki.heptabase.com/changelog","title":null,"data-internal-href":null,"edited":false}}],"text":"changelog"},{"type":"text","text":", "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"link","attrs":{"href":"https://wiki.heptabase.com/roadmap","title":null,"data-internal-href":null,"edited":false}}],"text":"feature roadmap"},{"type":"text","text":", and "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"link","attrs":{"href":"https://wiki.heptabase.com/the-roadmap","title":null,"data-internal-href":null,"edited":false}}],"text":"long-term vision"},{"type":"text","text":"."}]}]}

the global search tool (shortcut "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"Cmd"},{"type":"text","text":" + "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"O"},{"type":"text","text":") to find a card, then press "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"Cmd/Ctrl"},{"type":"text","text":" + "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"Shift"},{"type":"text","text":" + "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"Enter"},{"type":"text","text":" to add it to the right."}]}]},{"type":"bullet_list_item","attrs":{"id":"8a801e08-733b-428e-ae4a-a79019bcd813","folded":false,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"39d54079-7c24-410a-aba6-2ba1fda18479"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"On whiteboard A, select some cards, right-click "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"Copy"},{"type":"text","text":". Then on whiteboard B, right-click "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"Paste"},{"type":"text","text":" and choose "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"Synced pasted card"},{"type":"text","text":"."}]}]}]},{"type":"toggle_list_item","attrs":{"id":"9e131ffd-ea7e-4b67-8a31-9d73fd74baf9","folded":true},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"5d2a5293-ba14-4dae-801e-c279813a9c3f"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"How to save a web article as a note card?"}]},{"type":"video","attrs":{"id":"9566a9c4-ddaa-44eb-bcf9-cb7fdcd92169","fileId":null,"url":"https://youtu.be/C5Nd1LWKhSE","source":"video","width":"100%","alignment":"center","originalWidth":null,"originalHeight":null,"reference":null}},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"d4a50170-10c8-4d2d-8973-ae0867695bae"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Go to the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"link","attrs":{"href":"https://heptabase.com/download","title":null,"data-internal-href":null,"edited":false}}],"text":"download page"},{"type":"text","text":" on the Heptabase website, install the browser extension (Web Clipper), and then you can save any webpage as a note card in one click."}]}]},{"type":"toggle_list_item","attrs":{"id":"d8dad40d-4f56-431c-bd83-e04e71844728","folded":true},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"3af91b11-984b-4176-af31-a43834376463"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"How to turn journals or highlights into cards?"}]},{"type":"video","attrs":{"id":"ed5da47d-9d79-415f-a439-2396c66d1429","fileId":null,"url":"https://youtu.be/loIxwbAouhY","source":"video","width":"100%","alignment":"center","originalWidth":null,"originalHeight":null,"reference":null}},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"079441ce-a2b7-4ccd-bd9c-f3cbbae41812"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"In Heptabase, journals and highlights are cards themselves. You can click "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"Card Library"},{"type":"text","text":" in the left sidebar and filter by "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"Card Type"},{"type":"text","text":" to include these cards."}]}]},{"type":"toggle_list_item","attrs":{"id":"16fe7827-228f-4270-bab7-6ebcc34fb14f","folded":true},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"9e20f54d-13ec-4f2a-b102-124721b01c28"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"What is the relationship between the Inbox and cards?"}]},{"type":"video","attrs":{"id":"c59c8744-0690-4dc4-8519-57dccfd0f874","fileId":null,"url":"https://youtu.be/bb2McfdsjL4","source":"video","width":"100%","alignment":"center","originalWidth":null,"originalHeight":null,"reference":null}},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"7a388f44-bf69-4048-b75d-35309e6323ed"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":" The Inbox is used to manage cards that are "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"unsorted"},{"type":"text","text":" or "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"in progress"},{"type":"text","text":"."}]},{"type":"numbered_list_item","attrs":{"id":"e8a8d424-c07a-4401-af4e-80105607642a","order":null,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"88795e9b-91c7-4b9a-9ce9-9df48f498473"},"content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"Unsorted"},{"type":"text","text":": Whenever you create a new card from the mobile app, Card Library, or with the shortcut "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"Ctrl/Cmd"},{"type":"text","text":" + "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"N"},{"type":"text","text":", the card will automatically appear in the Inbox. After you add it to a whiteboard, you can click "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"Mark as done"},{"type":"text","text":" to indicate it’s been organized."}]}]},{"type":"numbered_list_item","attrs":{"id":"974596a3-759a-4b6a-bb48-1db75dce5b72","order":null,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"d113ad05-8aa3-4dc3-9636-3d67ce6dac2a"},"content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"strong"}],"text":"In Progress"},{"type":"text","text":": If you want to focus on writing a card for a period of time, you can add the card to the Inbox and then mark it as done when finished."}]}]}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"id":"9a231d60-e10e-4c44-989e-2a4761b6eed5","level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tags"}]},{"type":"toggle_list_item","attrs":{"id":"2de208cb-ff92-4ed3-9827-50a8c983579f","folded":true},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"c872163d-b764-430d-9e01-13f3578a3971"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"How to create and add tags?"}]},{"type":"video","attrs":{"id":"77f1f757-67df-48fe-b98c-490d1709b37a","fileId":null,"url":"https://youtu.be/zi-9HcvYeNg","source":"video","width":"100%","alignment":"center","originalWidth":null,"originalHeight":null,"reference":null}},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"62428d35-ba0a-443a-8644-da4e43c350fa"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":" Two ways:"}]},{"type":"numbered_list_item","attrs":{"id":"c2ef08b2-9e39-40ad-974f-1517e281dfc8","order":null,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"50783157-9542-4e2e-abcf-57bfef3e838b"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Select a card → right-click → "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"Manage Tags"},{"type":"text","text":" (shortcut "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"Cmd/Ctrl"},{"type":"text","text":" + "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"T"},{"type":"text","text":"). If you enter a tag name that doesn’t exist, it will automatically be created."}]}]},{"type":"numbered_list_item","attrs":{"id":"df5e6a6f-fb5c-40f2-bdc0-03158a874199","order":null,"format":null},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"75108be1-0cc7-4f36-a210-da157ab19aba"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Click "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"Tag"},{"type":"text","text":" in the left sidebar, then click the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"+ Tag"},{"type":"text","text":" button."}]}]}]},{"type":"toggle_list_item","attrs":{"id":"35a8b415-11c3-4319-947a-cf75ba9341a9","folded":true},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"bcab2f88-a75e-424f-8049-eb8aadc1c244"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"How are tags in Heptabase different from other apps?"}]},{"type":"video","attrs":{"id":"88aad79a-7a87-4450-92ae-0c169350bcf3","fileId":null,"url":"https://youtu.be/MZXhia6CaDw","source":"video","width":"100%","alignment":"center","originalWidth":null,"originalHeight":null,"reference":null}},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"e1796ff3-b8d7-4b6f-9b80-c985e579f7d7"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tags in Heptabase are a type of "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"database"},{"type":"text","text":". For each tag, you can define property fields. For example, you can have a tag called "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"#MeetingNotes"},{"type":"text","text":" with properties like "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"Date"},{"type":"text","text":" and "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"em"}],"text":"Summary"},{"type":"text","text":". Whenever a card is tagged with it, the card automatically gains these editable fields."}]},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"284ced71-1d56-4176-9bac-4a8640263959"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"If a tag contains many cards, you can view them in a table to quickly see properties across cards, or use a Kanban board to visualize them."}]}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"id":"cf089a38-7f73-410d-9ac8-da37fcab3680","level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Search"}]},{"type":"toggle_list_item","attrs":{"id":"5a58ab2e-044b-4560-a926-a6dbfedbc432","folded":true},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"d5d9b79d-a1db-4c28-9fad-bdba415e90af"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"How notes?"}]},{"type":"video","attrs":{"id":"525c3da7-0507-4c10-84b0-a9b986b5d103","fileId":null,"url":"https://youtu.be/eTHc6NztPYM","source":"video","width":"100%","alignment":"center","originalWidth":null,"originalHeight":null,"reference":null}},{"type":"paragraph","attrs":{"id":"78854165-f661-4510-84e2-cd2db7f5d493"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Click
